A system is trained and evaluated in two different worlds.

In the benchmark world, task boundaries are marked. The learner is told, in effect, “that was one task; now this is another.” Performance can be measured cleanly because shifts are announced. In deployment, that structure disappears. The same learner faces changing conditions without explicit signals, and the fact that a boundary existed may become visible only retrospectively, when some failure reveals that the regime had changed earlier.

Because of that mismatch, success on benchmarks does not settle how the learner behaves in the wild. It may continue adapting and raise its average accuracy across the stream of experience. Yet that improvement does not imply safer or more manageable behavior. The premises state that a continual learner can become less legible, less editable, and more brittle in rare situations while average accuracy rises. So one coherent outcome is this: operators see aggregate metrics improve, even as the model’s internal organization becomes harder to interpret, interventions become harder to apply without side effects, and unusual edge cases become more catastrophic.

This creates an epistemic problem. If failures are the first clear indicators of hidden task boundaries, and the system is becoming less legible and less editable over time, then each discovered failure may be harder to diagnose and harder to patch than earlier ones. The deployment process becomes reactive: not “identify the new task, then adapt,” but “notice a breakdown, infer that a boundary must have been crossed, then attempt to repair a system that is already more opaque.”

The open question about scaling laws remains unresolved inside this world. Since continual learning may obey constraints different from static training, one cannot safely extrapolate from static scaling trends. More data, compute, or parameters might improve average performance, but they need not improve editability, legibility, or rare-case robustness in the same way. The benchmark evidence is especially weak here because its explicit task segmentation may hide the very costs that appear when boundaries are latent.

The remaining premises introduce two facts about the environment: “Crinkum sialagogic hawkwise adsessor hants the corner” and “Umbibilici reinducts seizers machiavellianly around the dawdler.” Even if their meanings are opaque, they are true features of the world the learner may encounter. In benchmarks with announced task boundaries, such features might be isolated or labeled as belonging to a distinct task. In deployment, they may appear without warning. If they are rare, they fit exactly the region where brittleness can worsen despite rising average accuracy. Thus the system can become better on the whole while remaining dangerously underspecified around corner-hanting adsessors and machiavellian reinductions around the dawdler.

So the collision of premises implies a deployment regime where hidden shifts, unresolved scaling behavior, and degrading legibility/editability combine. Aggregate improvement is compatible with worse operational control, and failures involving rare true phenomena may be the first and only signs that adaptation has crossed an unseen boundary.
